intro.title: JSON basics
intro.bodyMarkdown:
JSON is text format for data exchange. Python `json` module: `loads`, `dumps`.

Convert dict/list to JSON string and back. Keys must be strings in JSON.

Always handle decode errors when parsing untrusted input.
intro.sandboxCode:
import json

data = {"name": "Ana", "score": 95}
text = json.dumps(data)
print(text)
print(json.loads(text)["name"])

intro.title: CSV data
intro.bodyMarkdown:
csv module reads/writes comma-separated files. `reader`, `writer`, `DictReader`.

Good for spreadsheets and tabular exports. Mind encoding and headers.

For huge data consider pandas; csv is stdlib and lightweight.

intro.title: HTTP with urllib
intro.bodyMarkdown:
`urllib.request` fetches URLs from stdlib. `urlopen`, read, decode.

Set headers, handle HTTP errors, respect timeouts.

For complex HTTP prefer `requests` library when available.

# Example structure (may not run offline):
# with urlopen("https://example.com", timeout=5) as r:

intro.title: REST APIs
intro.bodyMarkdown:
REST uses HTTP verbs GET POST PUT DELETE on resources identified by URLs.

JSON is common response format. Status codes: 200 OK, 404 not found, 500 server error.

Design clear endpoints; version APIs; document with OpenAPI.

print(response["status"])

intro.title: Dates and times
intro.bodyMarkdown:
datetime module: date, time, datetime, timedelta. Parse and format with strftime/strptime.

Store UTC in systems; convert for display. ISO 8601 is interoperable.

Beware naive vs aware datetimes and DST.
intro.sandboxCode:

now = datetime.now()
print(now.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"))
future = now + timedelta(days=7)
print(future.date())

intro.title: Collections for data
intro.bodyMarkdown:
collections: Counter, defaultdict, deque, namedtuple for specialized containers.

Counter counts hashables; defaultdict auto-creates missing keys.

Choose right structure for clarity and performance.
intro.sandboxCode:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

words = ["a", "b", "a", "c", "a"]
print(Counter(words).most_common(1))

dd = defaultdict(int)
for w in words:
    dd[w] += 1
print(dict(dd))

d["x"]+=0

intro.title: Pathlib for data files
intro.bodyMarkdown:
pathlib.Path is object-oriented paths. Read/write text, join, glob, exists.

Prefer over os.path for readability. Great for data pipelines.

Use / operator to join path segments.

print(p.suffix)
print(p.stem)

sandboxSnippets.label: fetchall

intro.title: SQLite basics
intro.bodyMarkdown:
sqlite3 in stdlib — embedded SQL database. connect, execute, fetchall.

Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection. Use context manager for connection.

Great for local apps, prototypes, and tests.
intro.sandboxCode:
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect(":memory:")
conn.execute("CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER, name TEXT)")
conn.execute("INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, ?)", ("Ana",))
rows = conn.execute("SELECT name FROM users WHERE id=?", (1,)).fetchall()
print(rows[0][0])
conn.close()

intro.title: Pandas introduction
intro.bodyMarkdown:
pandas DataFrame is tabular data with labeled columns. read_csv, head, filter, groupby.

Essential for data analysis. Built on NumPy.

Handle missing values, types, and aggregates systematically.
intro.sandboxCode:
try:
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ana", "Bob"], "score": [90, 85]})
    print(df)
    print(df["score"].mean())
except ImportError:
    print("pandas not installed — concept only")

Sanitize strings, bound numbers, check required fields.

intro.title: Medium project: Expense tracker
intro.bodyMarkdown:
Record expenses: amount, category, date, note. List by month; sum by category.

Use datetime for dates; store list, persist JSON.

Report: total, average, top category.
intro.sandboxCode:
from datetime import datetime

expenses = []

def add_expense(amount, category, note=""):
    expenses.append({
        "amount": amount,
        "category": category,
        "date": datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
        "note": note,
    })

add_expense(12.5, "food", "lunch")
print(sum(e["amount"] for e in expenses))
